stem:
A 27-year-old G2P1 at 39+2 weeks gestation is in your delivery suite in active second stage of labour with vertex presentation. The midwife performs a vaginal examination after a sudden gush of fluid and calls you saying 'there's a pulsating loop of cord palpable past the vertex and the foetal heart rate has dropped'. The cervix is fully dilated and the head is at station plus 1.

On examination she is alert and lying on her back. She has had an uncomplicated antenatal course apart from gestational hypertension controlled on labetalol. Her last vaginal examination 30 minutes ago showed full dilatation and station plus 1. The CTG had been reactive prior to the current event. The on-call obstetric consultant and anaesthetist are 3 minutes away. The theatre is empty and ready for a category 1 caesarean if needed. The bladder is empty. Her epidural is functioning well with adequate sensory block to the umbilicus.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 108, BP 134/82 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C, foetal HR 70 bpm sustained
data_table.Examination: Cervix fully dilated, head at station plus 1, pulsating cord palpable past the vertex, intact membranes ruptured
data_table.Investigations: CTG previously reactive, now sustained bradycardia foetal HR 70 bpm
data_table.Medications: Epidural with adequate block to umbilicus

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Place the mother in steep Trendelenburg position and arrange transfer to theatre for category 1 caesarean section
options.rationale: Category 1 caesarean is the default route for cord prolapse with foetal compromise when birth is not imminent, but when the cervix is fully dilated with low station, instrumental vaginal birth may be faster and is the preferred route here. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Statement)

options.text: Manually elevate the presenting part with a gloved hand and fill the bladder with 500-700 mL of saline
options.rationale: Manual elevation and bladder filling are correct temporising measures when caesarean is the planned route, but when the cervix is fully dilated with the head at +1, instrumental vaginal birth is faster than transfer to theatre. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Statement)

options.text: Proceed to immediate instrumental vaginal birth with vacuum or forceps assistance in the delivery room now
options.rationale: Cord prolapse with a fully dilated cervix and low station (+1 or lower) in an effective epidural with cooperative mother allows operative vaginal birth to be quicker than caesarean. Delivery time is the prime determinant of foetal outcome; the fastest safe route is the right route. Instrumental delivery is the AU recommended approach in this configuration. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Statement; RANZCOG Instrumental Vaginal Birth Statement)

options.text: Push the cord back above the presenting part and continue active second stage with directed pushing efforts
options.rationale: Attempting to replace the cord is associated with vasospasm and worsening foetal compromise; the cord is kept warm and unhandled while the route of delivery is decided. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Statement)

options.text: Administer intravenous terbutaline 250 microgram tocolysis and arrange urgent magnetic resonance imaging
options.rationale: Tocolysis is used to facilitate transfer to theatre when caesarean is the planned route; imaging has no place in acute cord prolapse management. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Statement)

explanation.summary: Umbilical cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency. Management hinges on the fastest safe route to deliver the baby and minimise cord compression. When the cervix is not fully dilated, category 1 caesarean is the route, with concurrent manual elevation of the presenting part, knee-chest or steep Trendelenburg position, bladder filling 500-700 mL, and tocolysis (terbutaline 250 microgram subcutaneous) to facilitate transfer. When the cervix is fully dilated with low station and an effective epidural, operative vaginal birth is faster and preferred. The cord is kept warm and unhandled to avoid vasospasm.
key_points: Fastest safe route to delivery is the principle
Not fully dilated: category 1 caesarean with temporising measures
Fully dilated and low station: instrumental vaginal birth
Cord kept warm and unhandled to avoid vasospasm
explanation.pearls: Bladder filling 500-700 mL via a Foley catheter elevates the presenting part and reduces cord compression more reliably than manual elevation alone when transfer to theatre is anticipated.

stem:
A 31-year-old G1P0 is referred to your foetal medicine clinic at 29+3 weeks gestation for severe early-onset foetal growth restriction. She has had one prior morphology scan at 20 weeks (normal) and a growth scan at 28 weeks showed estimated foetal weight at the 2nd centile. She has had no antepartum haemorrhage and no abdominal pain. Her booking visit blood pressure was 110/70 mmHg.

The current scan today shows estimated foetal weight 850 g (under 3rd centile), abdominal circumference under 3rd centile, head circumference at the 25th centile, amniotic fluid index 4 cm (deepest pool 2 cm), umbilical artery Doppler with absent end-diastolic flow, middle cerebral artery pulsatility index 0.78 (under 5th centile for gestation), and ductus venosus a-wave absent. She has had a normal full antenatal screen for infection and a normal karyotype. CTG shows reduced short-term variability over a 60-minute computerised assessment. Her blood pressure is 142/92 mmHg today and a spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio is 36 mg/mmol.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 92, BP 142/92 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C, foetal HR 142 bpm
data_table.Examination: Symphysio-fundal height 24 cm, soft abdomen, no tenderness
data_table.Investigations: EFW 850 g under 3rd centile; AC under 3rd centile; AFI 4 cm; UA Doppler absent end-diastolic flow; MCA PI 0.78; ductus venosus a-wave absent; computerised CTG reduced STV; urine PCR 36 mg/mmol
data_table.Medications: Two doses intramuscular betamethasone 11.4 mg given 24 and 12 hours ago; intravenous magnesium sulfate infusion started 4 hours ago for neuroprotection

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue inpatient observation with twice-daily computerised CTG and Doppler in 1 week for ongoing monitoring
options.rationale: Absent or reversed ductus venosus a-wave with reduced short-term variability on computerised CTG signals imminent decompensation; weekly review is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG IUGR Statement)

options.text: Wait until 30 weeks completed gestation before commencing any plan for delivery for additional foetal maturity
options.rationale: Ductus venosus a-wave loss combined with reduced computerised CTG STV at 29+3 weeks crosses the threshold for delivery; waiting risks intrauterine demise. (Source: TRUFFLE study)

options.text: Proceed to category 2 caesarean section once magnesium sulfate neuroprotection is complete with neonatal team in attendance
options.rationale: The TRUFFLE trial and RANZCOG IUGR guidance use ductus venosus a-wave (absent or reversed) plus reduced short-term variability on computerised CTG as the trigger for delivery in severe early-onset IUGR. Betamethasone is complete and magnesium sulfate for foetal neuroprotection has been started; once the second dose interval is met, category 2 caesarean delivery with neonatal team in attendance is the route. (Source: RANZCOG IUGR Statement; TRUFFLE study)

options.text: Induce labour with oral mifepristone followed by a vaginal prostaglandin pessary and aim for vaginal birth
options.rationale: Severe IUGR with absent end-diastolic flow and ductus venosus a-wave loss does not tolerate labour; the route of delivery is caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG IUGR Statement)

options.text: Treat the maternal blood pressure with labetalol and reassess foetal status after 48 hours of antihypertensive control
options.rationale: Maternal blood pressure control is warranted but does not change the foetal trigger; delivery is the response to the foetal indication. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Early-onset severe foetal growth restriction is the highest-risk subset for stillbirth and severe perinatal morbidity. The TRUFFLE trial established ductus venosus a-wave waveform changes plus reduced short-term variability on computerised CTG as superior to umbilical artery Doppler alone for delivery timing. RANZCOG triggers delivery when both criteria are met after corticosteroid completion and magnesium sulfate for foetal neuroprotection (under 30 weeks). Caesarean section is the route because severe IUGR foetuses tolerate labour poorly. Magnesium for neuroprotection is given for delivery at or before 30 weeks (some units extend to 32 weeks).
key_points: TRUFFLE trigger: ductus venosus a-wave absent/reversed plus reduced CTG STV
Magnesium sulfate for foetal neuroprotection at or before 30 weeks
Betamethasone before delivery at gestations less than 34+6 weeks
Caesarean is the preferred route in severe IUGR with abnormal Dopplers
explanation.pearls: Umbilical artery absent end-diastolic flow alone allows surveillance with delivery by 33+6; ductus venosus a-wave absent or reversed shifts delivery forward regardless of gestation between 26 and 32 weeks.

stem:
You are the obstetric registrar in delivery suite. A 34-year-old G3P3 has just delivered a 4.1 kg baby vaginally. Active management of the third stage was given. The midwife was applying cord traction when there was a sudden gush of blood and the mother became pale and clammy. The midwife says 'the fundus has disappeared and I can feel something like a bag at the introitus'.

On examination she is conscious but pale and diaphoretic. There is a large dark red mass visible at the introitus. The uterine fundus is no longer palpable abdominally. Estimated blood loss is now 1500 mL and increasing. She is in significant pain. Intravenous access has been established with a 16 G cannula and 0.9% sodium chloride is running. The anaesthetic registrar is in the room. The placenta is not yet delivered and appears still attached to the inverted uterine fundus. Bedside coagulation is pending. Bedside lactate is 3.6 mmol/L (under 2.0).

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 128 thready, BP 78/42 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 99% on 6 L/min mask oxygen, temperature 36.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Conscious pale diaphoretic, large dark red mass at the introitus, no fundus palpable abdominally, placenta still attached to inverted fundus
data_table.Investigations: FBC and coags sent, bedside lactate 3.6 mmol/L (under 2.0)
data_table.Medications: Active third stage included intramuscular oxytocin 10 IU; 0.9% sodium chloride running through 16 G cannula

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management priority?

options.text: Cease all uterotonics, leave the placenta attached, and immediately attempt manual replacement of the inverted fundus
options.rationale: Acute uterine inversion is reduced immediately by manual replacement (Johnson manoeuvre) WITHOUT removing the placenta first; placental removal before reduction worsens haemorrhage. Uterotonics are STOPPED to allow uterine relaxation; tocolysis (terbutaline subcutaneous, magnesium sulfate, or general anaesthesia with halothane) is added if simple manual replacement fails. Once reduced, uterotonics are restarted, the placenta is removed, and PPH management continues. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Statement; RANZCOG Acute Uterine Inversion Guideline)

options.text: Remove the placenta manually first to reduce blood loss, then attempt replacement of the inverted fundus
options.rationale: Removing the placenta from the inverted fundus before reduction sharply increases haemorrhage; the placenta is left in situ until after reduction. (Source: RANZCOG Acute Uterine Inversion Guideline)

options.text: Commence intravenous ergometrine 250 microgram before any attempt at manual reduction of the uterine fundus
options.rationale: Uterotonics increase uterine tone and oppose reduction; they are STOPPED for inversion. The sequence is reduce first, then uterotonics. (Source: RANZCOG Acute Uterine Inversion Guideline)

options.text: Immediately transfer to theatre for emergency abdominal hysterectomy under general anaesthesia as the first step
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is reserved for irreducible inversion or major haemorrhage uncontrolled by reduction and uterotonics; first-line is manual replacement. (Source: RANZCOG Acute Uterine Inversion Guideline)

options.text: Apply firm bimanual compression with two hands and administer intramuscular carboprost 250 microgram immediately
options.rationale: Bimanual compression and carboprost treat ongoing atony AFTER the inversion is reduced; before reduction they impede the manoeuvre. (Source: RANZCOG Acute Uterine Inversion Guideline)

explanation.summary: Acute uterine inversion is a rare but life-threatening obstetric emergency, classically presenting with maternal collapse, haemorrhage out of proportion to the visible loss, disappearing fundus on abdominal palpation, and a mass visible or palpable at the introitus. Treatment priority is immediate manual replacement (Johnson manoeuvre) with placenta still attached; uterotonics are STOPPED to allow uterine relaxation. If simple manual replacement fails, tocolysis is added (terbutaline 250 microgram subcutaneous, magnesium sulfate, or general anaesthesia with halothane) and a second attempt is made. Once reduced, uterotonics are restarted, placenta is delivered, and PPH management continues. Resuscitation runs in parallel.
key_points: Stop uterotonics; reduce first, then deliver placenta
Johnson manoeuvre: cup the fundus and push it back through the cervix
Tocolysis (terbutaline/magnesium/halothane) for failed first attempt
Hysterectomy for irreducible inversion or uncontrolled haemorrhage
explanation.pearls: The disappearance of the uterine fundus on abdominal palpation in a recently delivered woman with collapse is pathognomonic of inversion; vagal-mediated bradycardia can accompany the haemodynamic collapse.

stem:
A 33-year-old G2P1 at 37+0 weeks gestation is on your delivery suite for a planned induction. She had a velamentous cord insertion identified at her 20-week morphology scan with vasa praevia confirmed on transvaginal scan at 32 weeks. Antenatal corticosteroids were given at 32 and 34 weeks, and she has been admitted as an inpatient since 34 weeks for monitoring.

A few minutes after artificial rupture of membranes by the registrar (at 4 cm cervical dilation), the midwife reports a small painless gush of bright red blood. CTG immediately shows a sinusoidal trace with reduced variability and a baseline of 130 bpm. The previously reactive trace before ARM had a baseline of 140 bpm with moderate variability. Maternal observations remain stable. Theatre is staffed and the anaesthetic team is already in the room. Her booking blood group is O positive with no antibodies. She has had no prior caesarean and her epidural is functioning.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 92, BP 124/78 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C, foetal HR 130 bpm sinusoidal
data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm dilated, membranes ruptured, small bright red painless gush ongoing, foetal head at station 0
data_table.Investigations: CTG sinusoidal trace; Kleihauer test sent; FBC, group and hold available
data_table.Medications: Two doses antenatal betamethasone 11.4 mg at 32 and 34 weeks; functioning epidural

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Continue induction of labour and aim for vaginal birth with continuous CTG monitoring and operative delivery if needed
options.rationale: Vasa praevia bleeding is foetal blood loss and the foetal exsanguination window is minutes; vaginal birth is not safe once vasa praevia has bled. (Source: RANZCOG Vasa Praevia Statement)

options.text: Administer maternal intravenous tranexamic acid 1 g and arrange foetal blood transfusion in the neonatal unit
options.rationale: Maternal tranexamic acid does not address foetal bleeding from a ruptured foetal vessel; the only effective treatment is immediate delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Vasa Praevia Statement)

options.text: Perform manual digital tamponade against the bleeding vessel and observe CTG for spontaneous recovery over 15 minutes
options.rationale: Digital tamponade is not a recognised treatment and delays delivery; foetal exsanguination occurs within minutes once the vessel ruptures. (Source: RANZCOG Vasa Praevia Statement)

options.text: Activate the category 1 caesarean section pathway with delivery within 30 minutes and neonatal team in attendance
options.rationale: Vasa praevia with bleeding and a sinusoidal foetal heart trace is a category 1 caesarean indication; the bleeding is foetal and exsanguination occurs within minutes (foetal blood volume is approximately 80-100 mL/kg). Delivery within 30 minutes is the goal, neonatal resuscitation including O-negative crossmatched blood is prepared, and the baby is delivered by caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG Vasa Praevia Statement)

options.text: Send urgent Kleihauer test and Apt test for foetal blood differentiation before deciding mode of delivery
options.rationale: Kleihauer and Apt tests confirm foetal origin retrospectively; the diagnosis is clinical (known vasa praevia + bleeding after ARM + sinusoidal trace) and delivery cannot wait for laboratory confirmation. (Source: RANZCOG Vasa Praevia Statement)

explanation.summary: Vasa praevia is a foetal vessel running through the membranes within 2 cm of the internal cervical os, unsupported by placenta or cord. Risk factors include velamentous cord insertion, bilobed or succenturiate placenta, and twin pregnancy. Antenatal diagnosis by transvaginal Doppler is the standard. Confirmed cases are admitted between 30-34 weeks with antenatal corticosteroids and delivered by elective caesarean between 34-36 weeks before labour. Acute bleeding from ruptured vasa praevia is foetal in origin and produces rapid exsanguination; the foetal heart rate becomes sinusoidal and then bradycardic. Category 1 caesarean section is the only effective treatment. Neonatal resuscitation includes O-negative crossmatched blood for the baby.
key_points: Vasa praevia: foetal vessel within 2 cm of internal os, unsupported
Antenatal admission 30-34 weeks; delivery by elective caesarean 34-36 weeks
Acute rupture: category 1 caesarean within 30 minutes
Neonatal O-negative blood preparation for foetal exsanguination
explanation.pearls: Foetal blood volume is approximately 80-100 mL/kg; a foetus can exsanguinate within minutes of vasa praevia rupture, making this one of the few true obstetric emergencies with a minute-by-minute window.

stem:
A 29-year-old G1P1 woman is brought to your emergency department day 4 after a normal vaginal birth at term. Her partner says 'she's been getting more confused all day, her tummy is rock hard and she's gone really cold and shaky'. She had a brief sore throat the day before delivery. There is no history of antenatal infection and her group B streptococcus screen was negative.

On examination she is drowsy (GCS 13), with cool peripheries and mottled lower limbs. She has marked uterine tenderness, foul-smelling pinkish lochia, and a rigid abdomen. There is no breast erythema. Bedside lactate is 5.8 mmol/L (under 2.0); white cell count is 28 x10^9/L (4-11) with 92% neutrophils; CRP is 320 mg/L (under 5); creatinine has risen from 60 to 132 micromol/L. Bedside ultrasound shows retained products of conception 32 mm. She is exclusively breastfeeding a healthy term baby. The pelvic examination reveals an open cervical os with passage of foul-smelling discharge.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 138, BP 78/40 mmHg, RR 28, SpO2 95% on room air, temperature 39.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy GCS 13, cool peripheries with mottled lower limbs, rigid abdomen with uterine tenderness, foul-smelling lochia, open cervical os
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 5.8 mmol/L (under 2.0), WCC 28 x10^9/L (4-11) 92% neutrophils, CRP 320 mg/L (under 5), creatinine 132 micromol/L (45-90), retained products of conception 32 mm on bedside ultrasound

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Intravenous oral metronidazole monotherapy with intravenous fluids and observation for 6 hours
options.rationale: Monotherapy fails to cover gram-positive cocci including group A streptococcus, which is a leading cause of fulminant puerperal sepsis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin alone with intravenous fluids and immediate transfer to the postnatal ward
options.rationale: Penicillin alone does not cover anaerobes from retained products and lacks toxin suppression for group A streptococcus; broad-spectrum cover plus clindamycin is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g, metronidazole 500 mg, plus clindamycin 600 mg every 8 hours and urgent surgical evacuation
options.rationale: Postpartum septic shock with retained products in Australia is treated with broad gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobic cover plus clindamycin for its toxin-suppression effect on group A streptococcus and staphylococcal exotoxins. Therapeutic Guidelines lists ceftriaxone plus metronidazole plus clindamycin for severe puerperal sepsis with septic shock features. Source control by surgical evacuation of retained products is essential and is the most time-critical intervention. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; RANZCOG Puerperal Sepsis Statement)

options.text: Intravenous oral cephalexin and oral metronidazole pending culture results with outpatient follow-up in 48 hours
options.rationale: Oral therapy is inadequate for septic shock with end-organ dysfunction; intravenous broad-spectrum cover is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin alone for presumed methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus puerperal mastitis
options.rationale: This presentation is not lactational mastitis; vancomycin monotherapy lacks anaerobic and gram-negative cover required for endometritis and septic shock. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Puerperal sepsis is sepsis arising from a genital tract source within 6 weeks of birth, miscarriage, or termination of pregnancy. Group A streptococcus (GAS) is a leading cause of fulminant disease; sore throat exposure in the days before or after delivery is a classic risk factor. Antibiotic choice covers gram-positive cocci, gram-negatives, anaerobes, and adds clindamycin for toxin suppression. Therapeutic Guidelines specifies ceftriaxone plus metronidazole plus clindamycin for severe puerperal sepsis. Source control (surgical evacuation of retained products, drainage of pelvic collections, hysterectomy in irreversible necrotising endometritis) is the most time-critical intervention. Resuscitation runs in parallel with the hour-1 sepsis bundle.
key_points: Empirical IV ceftriaxone + metronidazole + clindamycin in severe puerperal sepsis
Clindamycin added for GAS and staphylococcal toxin suppression
Source control: surgical evacuation of retained products
Hour-1 sepsis bundle in parallel
explanation.pearls: In the breastfeeding patient, ceftriaxone, metronidazole, and clindamycin are all considered acceptable; brief lactation interruption is unnecessary in routine practice and exclusive expressed breastmilk feeding continues.

stem:
A 27-year-old woman presents to your general practice clinic on her own initiative requesting an autism assessment. She says 'I've masked all my life and I'm exhausted and I can't keep doing it'. She describes intense restricted interests since childhood (collecting and cataloguing fossils for 18 years), sensory sensitivities (overwhelmed in supermarkets, prefers seam-free clothing), and significant social exhaustion after work meetings. She has had a long-term partner for 5 years.

She was a high-achieving student through school and university (engineering degree, GPA 6.9/7) and has held the same software engineering job for 3 years. She struggles with workplace small talk and has had two episodes of generalised anxiety (one in second year of university, one this year) treated with cognitive behavioural therapy. She does not meet criteria for current depression (PHQ-9 = 6). Her father has been described as having 'quirky obsessive interests'. She has no history of self-injurious behaviour and her speech and motor milestones were normal in childhood. Her workplace has just transferred her to a new open-plan office, and she has been having panic attacks in the bathroom.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, cooperative, normal speech rhythm and prosody, brief intermittent eye contact, no abnormal movements
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 = 6 (not depressed), GAD-7 = 14 (moderate-severe anxiety), AQ-50 = 38 (above clinical threshold of 32)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Diagnose generalised anxiety disorder and commence sertraline 50 mg daily with cognitive behavioural therapy referral
options.rationale: Anxiety is a comorbidity and not a substitute for the autism assessment she has requested; anxiety treatment alone misses the developmental diagnosis. (Source: RANZCP CPG ASD; NICE Adult Autism Guideline)

options.text: Reassure her that high-functioning autism is unlikely given her professional success and partnered relationship status
options.rationale: High autonomic load with masking is characteristic of late-recognised autism in women; functional achievement does not exclude autism. (Source: RANZCP CPG ASD)

options.text: Refer to a specialist multidisciplinary diagnostic service experienced in adult autism in women for formal assessment
options.rationale: Adult autism assessment requires multidisciplinary specialist input including a structured developmental interview (ADI-R or equivalent) and an observational assessment (ADOS-2) with attention to female phenotype features (masking, intense restricted interests in socially accepted domains, internalising comorbidity). General practice screening with AQ-50 above threshold supports referral but does not constitute diagnosis. Concurrent anxiety management is appropriate but does not replace the diagnostic pathway. (Source: RANZCP CPG ASD)

options.text: Refer to occupational health for workplace accommodation under the Disability Discrimination Act before any diagnosis
options.rationale: Workplace accommodation under the DDA depends on a formal diagnosis being established or the worker self-identifying as having a disability; assessment is the prerequisite. (Source: Australian Human Rights Commission)

options.text: Initiate intranasal oxytocin trial for autism social cognition difficulties as the first-line pharmacological intervention
options.rationale: Intranasal oxytocin is not a TGA-approved or AU first-line treatment for autism; evidence is mixed and use is restricted to research settings. (Source: TGA)

explanation.summary: Adult autism in women is frequently identified late because of masking (consciously or unconsciously hiding autistic traits in social settings) and restricted interests aligned with socially accepted domains. Diagnosis is by specialist multidisciplinary assessment with a structured developmental interview (parent-informant if possible) and an observational measure. RANZCP guidance acknowledges the female phenotype and the high comorbidity with anxiety and depression. Management is post-diagnosis and includes psychoeducation, occupational input, sensory environment modification, and concurrent treatment of comorbid anxiety or depression. Late diagnosis often produces relief and improved self-understanding for the person.
key_points: Masking and internalising features common in women with late diagnosis
Multidisciplinary assessment with ADI-R/ADOS-2 (or AU equivalents)
High comorbidity with anxiety and depression
Workplace accommodation under DDA requires assessment first
explanation.pearls: The Autism Quotient (AQ-50) is a self-rated screening tool with a clinical cut-off at 32; sensitivity is reasonable but specificity is modest, so a positive screen prompts referral rather than confirming diagnosis.

stem:
A 51-year-old man presents to your general practice clinic for help quitting smoking. He has smoked 25 cigarettes per day for 33 years (pack-years over 40) and has tried unsupported quitting three times. He says 'I really want to stop for my grandkids, but I'm worried because I've been on antidepressants forever'. His major depressive disorder is currently in remission on sertraline 100 mg daily; the last episode was 2 years ago. He scores PHQ-9 = 3 today.

He has stable angina on metoprolol 25 mg twice daily and an ejection fraction of 60% on a recent echocardiogram. He has had no acute coronary syndrome in the last 12 months. He has no history of seizures, alcohol use disorder, or eating disorder. His BMI is 24. He has no suicidal ideation now and no past suicide attempts. He has tried nicotine replacement therapy in two of his prior attempts; he found the patch helpful but discontinued because of vivid dreams. He scores Fagerstrom = 7 (high dependence). Spirometry is mild obstruction (FEV1 78% predicted) with reversibility.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 64, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, cooperative, no acute distress, mild expiratory wheeze on auscultation
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 = 3 (remission); Fagerstrom = 7 (high dependence); FEV1 78% predicted with reversibility
data_table.Medications: Sertraline 100 mg daily, metoprolol 25 mg twice daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Bupropion 150 mg daily increasing to 150 mg twice daily after 1 week with target quit date in week 2
options.rationale: Bupropion is contraindicated in active seizure disorder and is best avoided in patients with reduced seizure threshold; varenicline has better cessation outcomes in EAGLES and is no longer considered to worsen neuropsychiatric outcomes. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation Guideline)

options.text: Combination nicotine replacement therapy patch plus lozenge as needed with behavioural support and weekly review
options.rationale: Combination NRT is reasonable second-line, but varenicline has the highest single-agent cessation success rates in the EAGLES trial population including stable depression. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation Guideline)

options.text: Nortriptyline 25 mg daily titrated to 75 mg as a TGA-approved off-label smoking cessation aid for resistant cases
options.rationale: Nortriptyline is a third-line agent; it has cardiac and anticholinergic effects that are less favourable than varenicline in stable depression. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation Guideline)

options.text: Clonidine 0.1 mg twice daily as the AU first-line cessation aid in a patient with comorbid hypertension and depression
options.rationale: Clonidine is fourth-line at best and is not the AU first-line; sedation, hypotension, and rebound limit its use. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation Guideline)

options.text: Varenicline starting 0.5 mg daily titrating to 1 mg twice daily over week 1 with quit date day 8 of treatment
options.rationale: Varenicline (alpha-4 beta-2 nicotinic partial agonist) is the AU first-line single agent with the highest cessation rates. The 2016 EAGLES trial demonstrated no increase in neuropsychiatric adverse events in patients with stable psychiatric disorders compared with placebo. Stable depression on SSRI is not a contraindication. The 12-week course is started 8 days before the planned quit date with titration to 1 mg twice daily. Smoking cessation does not reduce SSRI efficacy but smoking cessation does increase plasma levels of some psychotropics (clozapine, olanzapine) through CYP1A2; sertraline is unaffected. (Source: RACGP Supporting Smoking Cessation Guideline; EAGLES trial)

explanation.summary: Australian guidance ranks varenicline as the first-line single agent for nicotine dependence in adults able to take it. The 2016 EAGLES trial demonstrated comparable neuropsychiatric safety in stable psychiatric patients to non-psychiatric controls, leading to removal of the prior FDA boxed warning. Combination NRT (patch plus oral form) is the alternative first-line for those unable to tolerate varenicline. Bupropion is contraindicated in seizure disorder and bipolar disorder. Behavioural support amplifies pharmacotherapy effect. Smoking cessation increases plasma levels of clozapine, olanzapine, theophylline (CYP1A2 induction by tobacco smoke reverses on cessation) and dose review is required; sertraline is not affected.
key_points: Varenicline first-line; safe in stable depression per EAGLES
NRT combination as first-line alternative
Bupropion contraindicated in seizure disorder
Cessation increases CYP1A2 substrate levels (clozapine, olanzapine, theophylline)
explanation.pearls: Smoking cessation increases plasma clozapine concentration by 50-100% within 2-4 weeks through CYP1A2 de-induction; the clozapine dose is reduced by about 30% at cessation with weekly level monitoring.

stem:
A 22-year-old woman presents to your community mental health team for the third time in 6 months after intentional superficial cutting of her forearm. She says 'when my boyfriend pulls away I feel like I'm disappearing and only the cutting brings me back'. She has chronic feelings of emptiness, recurrent self-harm without suicidal intent, intense unstable relationships, and rapidly shifting mood states. She has been on three different SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram, fluoxetine) over 2 years without sustained benefit.

She has had two brief admissions for self-harm in the last year; she finds inpatient settings escalate rather than calm her. There is no current suicidal plan or intent. There is no current depression by PHQ-9 (= 9, mild). She uses cannabis on weekends and drinks 4 standard drinks twice a week. Her sleep is fragmented. She has no psychotic features and her reality testing is intact. Her family environment in childhood was emotionally invalidating with intermittent neglect; there is no formal trauma history at this assessment. Her GP has been prescribing repeat low-dose quetiapine 25 mg as needed for distress, which she uses two or three times a week.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, cooperative, mood labile, no psychomotor agitation, no thought disorder, multiple superficial healed forearm cuts
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 = 9 (mild), no current suicidal plan or intent; AUDIT-C = 4
data_table.Medications: Quetiapine 25 mg as needed (used 2-3 times per week); previously sertraline, escitalopram, fluoxetine

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Restart escitalopram 10 mg daily and titrate to 20 mg over 4 weeks aimed at chronic emotional dysregulation
options.rationale: SSRIs are not first-line for BPD core symptoms; the AU guidance reserves psychotropics for clear comorbid depression, anxiety, or short-term crisis use, and removes them for emotional dysregulation alone. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guidelines; RANZCP CPG Personality Disorder)

options.text: Admit to an inpatient unit for at least 2 weeks of structured behavioural therapy and medication review
options.rationale: Inpatient admission is reserved for acute crisis with imminent risk and short stays (commonly under 72 hours); prolonged admission risks worsening BPD outcomes. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Increase the as-needed quetiapine dose to 100 mg with broader availability for breakthrough distress
options.rationale: Routine as-needed antipsychotic for distress is discouraged; structured psychological therapy with crisis planning is the evidence-based treatment. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for structured psychological therapy (dialectical behaviour therapy or mentalisation-based therapy) with clear crisis plan
options.rationale: Australian NHMRC and RANZCP guidance position structured psychological therapy (DBT, MBT, schema-focused therapy, transference-focused therapy) as the cornerstone of BPD treatment. Pharmacotherapy is reserved for clearly defined comorbidities or short-term crisis use. A written crisis plan, safety planning, and a relationship with one primary treating clinician reduces hospitalisation and improves outcomes. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guidelines; RANZCP CPG Personality Disorder)

options.text: Commence lamotrigine 25 mg titrating to 200 mg over 8 weeks for affective instability in personality disorder
options.rationale: Recent trials (LABILE 2018) showed lamotrigine offers no advantage over placebo for BPD affective symptoms; the AU guidance does not recommend it as first-line. (Source: NHMRC BPD Clinical Practice Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Borderline personality disorder responds best to structured psychological therapy. Australian (NHMRC) guidance lists dialectical behaviour therapy, mentalisation-based therapy, schema-focused therapy, and transference-focused therapy as first-line. Pharmacotherapy is reserved for clearly defined comorbidities (current major depression, persistent generalised anxiety, post-traumatic stress disorder) or short-term crisis use; routine SSRI or antipsychotic use for emotional dysregulation alone is discouraged. A written crisis plan with the patient, a primary treating clinician, and a clear pathway for short focused admissions (commonly under 72 hours) is more effective than reactive long-stay admission. The recovery trajectory is more favourable than once thought, with majority remission of BPD criteria over a decade.
key_points: First-line: DBT, MBT, schema therapy, TFP
Pharmacotherapy reserved for comorbidity or short-term crisis
Brief admission only; long-stay risks worsening
Crisis plan, primary clinician, scheduled review
explanation.pearls: The LABILE trial (Lancet Psychiatry 2018) showed no benefit of lamotrigine over placebo for BPD affective symptoms; AU guidance has reduced routine use of mood stabilisers in this population.

subtopic_detail: Schizoaffective vs bipolar with psychotic features
stem:
A 26-year-old man is admitted to your inpatient psychiatry unit on an involuntary order. His sister says 'he's been hearing voices on and off for nearly a year now, even when he's not high or low'. His mood has alternated between two clear depressive episodes (3 and 7 months ago) and one clear manic episode (4 months ago, lasting 2 weeks with reduced sleep, grandiosity, hypersexuality, and pressured speech). Through this period he has had persistent auditory hallucinations of two male voices commenting on his actions, including at least 2 weeks of euthymia between episodes when the voices continued.

He has no substance use disorder. Urine drug screen is negative. There is no medical cause identified on workup (CT brain normal, TSH normal, B12 normal, syphilis serology negative, HIV negative). He has been started on olanzapine 10 mg with reasonable effect on the active psychotic symptoms and was previously on sertraline (currently off it). His insight is limited but improving. The family asks 'what is it actually, doctor?' and his sister insists 'he's not just bipolar'.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 122/74 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, cooperative, third-person auditory hallucinations described, normal speech rhythm, no thought disorder, mood currently euthymic
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen negative, CT brain normal, TSH 1.8 mU/L (0.5-4.0), B12 410 pmol/L (200-700), HIV and syphilis negative

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with psychotic features confined to mood episodes only and mood-congruent themes
options.rationale: Bipolar I with mood-congruent psychosis has psychosis only during mood episodes; this patient has 2 weeks or more of psychosis during euthymia, which excludes the diagnosis. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Substance-induced psychotic disorder with mood symptoms secondary to long-standing cannabis dependence
options.rationale: Urine drug screen is negative and the symptom pattern is not time-locked to substance use; substance-induced disorder requires temporal association with substance exposure. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Major depressive disorder with mood-congruent psychotic features in the context of repeated severe episodes
options.rationale: Recurrent severe major depression with psychosis fits some criteria but does not explain the manic episode, which qualifies the patient for bipolar spectrum or schizoaffective consideration. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type with mood and psychotic symptoms in defined temporal relationship
options.rationale: DSM-5-TR schizoaffective disorder requires: (1) major mood episode (depressive or manic) concurrent with criterion A of schizophrenia for an uninterrupted period; (2) delusions or hallucinations for 2 weeks or more in the absence of a major mood episode at some time during the lifetime duration; (3) major mood episode symptoms present for the majority of the active and residual illness duration; (4) not attributable to substance or medical cause. The 2-week euthymic interval with persistent psychosis distinguishes schizoaffective from bipolar with psychosis. Bipolar subtype is used because of the manic episode. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Schizophreniform disorder with associated mood disturbance from social and occupational consequences of psychosis
options.rationale: Schizophreniform disorder lasts under 6 months; this patient's illness has been at least 1 year and includes prominent mood episodes meeting full criteria. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder is distinguished from bipolar disorder with psychotic features by the presence of delusions or hallucinations for 2 weeks or more in the ABSENCE of a major mood episode at some point during the illness. Mood symptoms must be present for the majority of the active and residual illness duration (otherwise primary schizophrenia with mood symptoms is the diagnosis). Bipolar subtype includes manic episodes; depressive subtype includes only depressive episodes. Treatment requires both mood stabilisation (lithium, valproate, or atypical with mood stabilising effect such as quetiapine, olanzapine, aripiprazole) and antipsychotic effect, often via the same atypical agent.
key_points: 2+ weeks of psychosis in absence of mood episode at some point
Mood symptoms majority of active/residual illness duration
Bipolar subtype if manic episode present
Treatment: mood stabiliser plus antipsychotic effect
explanation.pearls: The 2-week criterion of psychosis without mood episode is the cleanest discriminator between schizoaffective disorder and bipolar with psychotic features; a careful longitudinal interval history is essential.

stem:
A 19-year-old university student is admitted to your psychiatry unit with a 4-week history of paranoid delusions (his housemates are spying on him through air vents), third-person auditory hallucinations, and thought broadcasting. His mother says 'he's been smoking strong skunk weed almost every day for the last 2 years'. He has been continuously using high-potency cannabis (estimated 16% THC) daily for 24 months. Urine THC and metabolites are positive on admission.

He was abstinent for 4 weeks during a remote bushwalking trip 6 months ago; his mother says the voices and paranoid ideas continued throughout that abstinent period and only briefly faded when he returned and started using again. His paternal uncle has schizophrenia. He has had functional decline over 12 months (left first-year engineering after one semester) and his negative symptoms (amotivation, social withdrawal, blunted affect) have persisted for 8 months. There is no acute mood episode (PHQ-9 = 7, YMRS = 3). His insight is poor.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Alert, paranoid, third-person auditory hallucinations, thought broadcasting, blunted affect, no abnormal movements
data_table.Investigations: Urine THC and metabolites positive, urine other drugs negative, CT brain normal, TSH normal, B12 normal

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Cannabis-induced psychotic disorder that will resolve with abstinence and supportive care alone
options.rationale: Cannabis-induced psychosis resolves within days to weeks of abstinence; persistent psychosis through 4 weeks of abstinence is inconsistent. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Schizotypal personality disorder with eccentric thinking exacerbated by chronic cannabis exposure
options.rationale: Schizotypal personality disorder does not include sustained delusions, hallucinations, or thought broadcasting; this patient's presentation fits frank psychotic illness. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder with substance-related precipitation expected to resolve fully within 4 weeks
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder lasts under 1 month; this patient's psychotic symptoms have been continuous over 8-12 months with negative symptoms and functional decline. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Major depressive disorder with cannabis use complicating mood and producing transient pseudo-psychotic features
options.rationale: His PHQ-9 is in the mild-to-moderate range and there is no acute mood episode; cannabis-related psychosis without mood prominence is not depression. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Primary schizophrenia (cannabis use as risk factor) with characteristic features sustained through abstinence
options.rationale: Continuous psychotic symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, thought broadcasting) through 4 weeks of cannabis abstinence, with functional decline over 12 months, sustained negative symptoms over 8 months, family history, and high-potency cannabis use as a risk factor, fits primary schizophrenia. Cannabis use is causally associated (genetic risk and high-potency THC exposure increase schizophrenia odds ratio approximately threefold) but does not redefine the diagnosis when symptoms persist beyond cannabis clearance. Treatment includes atypical antipsychotic and structured substance use disorder care. (Source: RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia; DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: Differentiating cannabis-induced psychosis from primary schizophrenia in a young user requires longitudinal assessment of symptoms during abstinence. Cannabis-induced psychotic disorder resolves within days to a few weeks of abstinence; persistent symptoms beyond 4 weeks of demonstrated abstinence, sustained negative symptoms, and functional decline support primary schizophrenia. Cannabis is a risk factor for schizophrenia onset, especially when high-potency, daily, and used in adolescence; the odds ratio is approximately threefold in heavy daily users. Treatment uses an atypical antipsychotic (aripiprazole, olanzapine, risperidone) plus structured substance use disorder management; cannabis cessation is the most modifiable risk for relapse.
key_points: Cannabis-induced psychosis: resolves within days-weeks of abstinence
Persistent psychosis beyond 4 weeks abstinence supports primary psychosis
Cannabis is risk factor (OR ~3) for schizophrenia in heavy users
Cessation is the most modifiable relapse risk
explanation.pearls: High-potency cannabis (>=10% THC) used daily in adolescence is associated with a dose-response increase in schizophrenia risk; an Australian young person presenting with first-episode psychosis should be screened with quantitative cannabis history.
